hook: Carl Jacobsen's sculpture museum wrapped around a glass-domed subtropical winter garden of palms and fountains.
facts: Founded by Carlsberg brewer Carl Jacobsen and opened to the public in stages from 1897, with the Dahlerup building (1897) and Kampmann's winter-garden extension (1906).
The Winter Garden, built in 1906, sits under a copper-and-wrought-iron dome and contains tall palm trees, a fountain and a pool.
The collection spans ancient Egyptian, Greek, Etruscan and Roman sculpture alongside 19th-century Danish and French art, including works by Rodin and a major group of Degas bronzes.
It holds one of the largest collections of antique Roman portrait busts outside the Mediterranean.
Address is Dantes Plads 7, 1556 Copenhagen V, between Tivoli Gardens and the harbour.
w: Carl Jacobsen spent his brewing fortune on marble, then wrapped the galleries around a glass-domed winter garden of palms and fountains. Beyond the dripping greenery wait Egyptian and Etruscan rooms, a wall of Roman portrait busts, and one of the largest hoards of Degas bronzes anywhere. Beer money, beautifully spent.

hook: Denmark's WWII resistance museum, rebuilt mostly underground in Churchill Park after an arson fire destroyed the old hall.
facts: Frihedsmuseet documents the Danish resistance movement during the German occupation of 1940-1945 and is part of the National Museum of Denmark.
The original museum building was destroyed by an arson fire on 28 April 2013.
A new building designed by Lundgaard & Tranberg Architects reopened in 2020, with most exhibition space placed below ground.
It stands in Churchillparken, just south of the Kastellet citadel, near Amalienborg and Langelinie.
Collections include artefacts of sabotage, illegal printing and the 1943 rescue of Danish Jews to Sweden.
w: Denmark's resistance museum burned down in an arson fire in 2013; its replacement sank most of itself underground in Churchill Park, by the Kastellet ramparts. Inside lie the sabotage tools, the illegal presses, and the small-boat rescue that ferried nearly all of Denmark's Jews to Sweden in 1943. Memory, rebuilt below grade.

hook: The world's largest collection of unopened bottled beer, tens of thousands strong, shelved in the basement of the old Carlsberg brewery.
facts: Engineer Leif Sonne of Svendborg began collecting unopened beer bottles as a hobby in 1968.
When the collection outgrew his house it was moved to the Carlsberg Brewery in Copenhagen in 1993.
By 2007 it numbered more than 22,000 different bottles and was certified by Guinness World Records as the largest collection of bottled beer in the world.
It is displayed in the basement of the brewery's visitor attraction, reopened as Home of Carlsberg in December 2023, at Ny Carlsberg Vej 100.
The attraction is open to the public daily, so the collection is freely visitable with admission.
w: Engineer Leif Sonne started squirreling away unopened beer bottles in 1968; by 2007 his hoard topped 22,000 and Guinness crowned it the world's largest. It outgrew his Svendborg house long ago and now fills a basement under the old Carlsberg brewery. Every bottle full, every cap still on, none of them his to drink.

hook: A single hotel room for two perched above a tiny café — billed as the world's smallest hotel.
facts: Central Hotel & Café opened in 2012 on Tullinsgade in Vesterbro and consists of a single guest room above a small ground-floor café.
The hotel room measures roughly 12 square metres (about 129 sq ft) and sleeps two.
It was created by Copenhagen restaurateur and former set designer Leif Thingtved, who furnished it in a vintage 1950s style.
The corner building housed a cobbler's shop in the early 1900s; the cobbler converted the attic into living space after the war.
The room and café occupy separate floors, and there is no on-site reception — the café operates by day while the single room is let by night.
legend: It is popularly promoted as 'the world's smallest hotel,' a claim the owner embraces as the venue's selling point.
w: One room, twelve square metres, two guests, no reception, and billed without apology as the world's smallest hotel. Set designer Leif Thingtved built it above a pocket cafe on Tullinsgade in 2012 and kitted it out in full 1950s style. The cobbler who once worked this corner would barely know his attic. Sleep cheaply, sleep small.

hook: A graffiti-walled basement dive on Larsbjørnsstræde, a survivor of Copenhagen's 1980s punk scene.
facts: Floss is a bodega-style dive bar at Larsbjørnsstræde 10, 1454 København K, in the Latin Quarter.
The downstairs room has graffiti-covered walls, a pool table and a football table, and indoor smoking is permitted.
It is known for cheap drinks, including a long-standing offer of ten tequila shots for 100 DKK.
It is open daily into the early hours, with listed hours around 13:00/14:00 until 02:00.
The bar is confirmed open as of 2026 via current Yelp and VisitCopenhagen listings.
legend: The bar is widely described as a holdover from the street's 1980s punk-rock club scene.
w: A graffiti-smeared cellar where the smoke still hangs indoors and ten tequilas cost a hundred kroner. Floss has clung to Larsbjornsstraede since the street's 1980s punk-club days, trading polish for a pool table, a football table and cheap shots into the small hours. A dive in the truest sense, and proud of it.

hook: Copenhagen's oldest bakery, famous for the giant 'Wednesday snail' cinnamon roll.
facts: Sankt Peders Bageri stands on Sankt Peders Stræde in the Latin Quarter and is described as Copenhagen's oldest bakery.
Its signature 'onsdagssnegl' (Wednesday snail) is an oversized cinnamon roll, roughly twice the size of a standard kanelsnegl.
The Wednesday snail debuted in 1988; the bakery sells around 4,000 of them every Wednesday.
Originally sold only on Wednesdays, the snail is now available all week but still discounted on Wednesdays.
The bakery building is protected/listed, and the interior includes two black chandeliers and a row of bar stools.
legend: The bakery is traditionally said to have been founded in 1652, during the reign of Frederick III.
w: Reckoned Copenhagen's oldest bakery and said to date to 1652, but Wednesdays are what people queue for. That's when the onsdagssnegl appears: a cinnamon snail twice the usual size, some 4,000 of them shifted in a day since 1988. Black chandeliers, a listed shopfront, and a roll worth setting an alarm for.

hook: An 1835 tea merchant on Kronprinsensgade, still run from the same listed shop seven generations on.
facts: A.C. Perch's Thehandel was founded in April 1835 by Niels Brock Perch at Kronprinsensgade 5 and named after his son Axel Christian Perch.
The shop building dates from 1805 and is a listed (protected) structure.
It is described as the eighth oldest shop in Copenhagen and has operated continuously as a tea merchant since 1835.
An English-style tearoom, Perch's Tea Room, opened on the floor above the shop in the mid-2000s.
The business is now owned by the Hincheldey family, who took it over in 1894.
w: Niels Brock Perch opened this tea merchant in 1835 and named it for his son Axel Christian; seven generations on it still weighs out leaves from the same listed 1805 shop. Reckoned the eighth-oldest business in the city, with an English tearoom tucked up the stairs. A place where the scales haven't changed, and nor has the smell.

hook: Cult burgers grilled inside a working retro petrol station off Borgergade.
facts: Gasoline Grill's original location is at Landgreven 10, 1301 København K, set in a former/retro petrol station.
The burger bar opened at the site in 2016 and built a reputation for selling out daily — it operates from 11:00 'until sold out.'
In 2017 Bloomberg included it on a list of 27 of the best burgers in the world.
Seating is outdoors only and reservations are not accepted.
The Landgreven site is slated to close (with the gas station demolished) for an underground parking renovation; as of June 2026 it remains open.
w: Cult burgers grilled inside a working retro filling station, served until the day's batch sells out, which it always does. Gasoline Grill opened at Landgreven in 2016 and landed on Bloomberg's best-in-the-world list a year later. No reservations, no indoor seats, no leftovers. Eat at the pumps while the original site still stands.

hook: A candlelit, cave-like basement café strewn with couches and Moroccan lanterns.
facts: The Living Room is a café and bar at Larsbjørnsstræde 17, 1454 København K, in the Latin Quarter.
The café takes its name from its basement level, furnished with leather couches and armchairs around a fireplace.
The candlelit basement is decorated with Moroccan lanterns, giving a cave-like, intimate atmosphere.
It keeps long hours, open daily from morning and late into the night on weekends.
It is confirmed open as of 2026 via current Yelp and VisitCopenhagen listings.
w: Climb down into a candlelit cellar of leather couches, a fireplace and a scatter of Moroccan lanterns, and the city above goes quiet. The Living Room earns its name on the basement floor, all cave-like hush and long opening hours. Coffee by day, something stronger by night, and a sofa you won't want to leave.

hook: A tiny blue harbour shack with a wood-fired sauna and a pier you can swim off, on Refshaleøen.
facts: La Banchina occupies a small wooden building at Refshalevej 141, on the waterfront of the former shipyard island Refshaleøen.
The building was an abandoned ferry waiting room for shipyard workers before being renovated by founder Christer Bredgaard.
It has a wood-fired sauna offering private sessions of roughly 1 hour 45 minutes, with swimming from its own pier in season.
It functions as a café and natural-wine bar serving coffee, baked goods, beer and low-intervention wines, with a mostly vegetarian and pescatarian kitchen.
The café is open daily year-round; the kitchen runs a reduced winter schedule (Thursday to Sunday).
w: A little blue shack on the Refshaleoen waterfront, once a ferry waiting room for shipyard workers, now a wood-fired sauna with a pier to plunge off. Sweat, swim, repeat, then dry out over coffee, natural wine and whatever the tiny kitchen is baking. Open all year, which is rather the point when the water turns cold.

hook: A tiny, flower-choked lane of crooked coloured houses on the edge of Nyboder, framed by Jerusalem's Church.
facts: The street is named after the mugwort/spearmint plant (krusemynte) and sits within the old Nyboder naval district laid out under Christian IV in the 17th century.
In the early 1870s the original Nyboder houses on the lane were demolished and replaced by two-storey workers' housing designed by architect Frederik Bøttger, the same pattern as the Potato Rows.
The houses were repainted in varied colours after an extensive renovation, giving the lane its present mismatched palette.
It is regularly cited among Copenhagen's most photogenic and 'hidden' streets, tucked off the main tourist routes in the city's north-eastern quarter.
w: A pocket lane named for a herb, choked with climbing flowers and lined with crooked, mismatched houses on the edge of Christian IV's old naval Nyboder. The original cottages came down in the 1870s for two-storey workers' housing, later repainted in clashing colours. Easy to walk past, hard to forget once you've found it.

hook: Rows of identical terraced workers' houses on streets named after sculptors, built on former potato fields between the Lakes and Sølvgade.
facts: The district comprises roughly 480 terraced houses built 1873–1889 by the Workers' Building Society (Arbejdernes Byggeforening) on English models, designed by architect Frederik Christian Bøttger.
The name 'Potato Rows' comes from the potato fields that occupied the land before construction.
It sits in the triangle between Øster Søgade, Øster Farimagsgade and Sølvgade; the parallel streets (e.g. Wiedeweltsgade, Webersgade) are largely named after Danish artists and sculptors.
Originally each three-storey house held two or three families, one per floor, housing on average around eight people per dwelling into the 1900s.
Residents successfully fought off a mid-20th-century plan for a motorway 'lake ring' over the adjacent lakes; the area is now among Copenhagen's most expensive and sought-after housing.
w: Built on old potato fields between 1873 and 1889, these 480-odd terraced houses gave Copenhagen's workers an English-style home of their own. The parallel rows take their names from Danish sculptors, and the residents once beat back a plan to run a motorway over the neighbouring lakes. Humble brick that's now some of the city's priciest.

hook: Long rows of ochre-and-white terraced cottages between Østerbrogade and Øster Allé — one of Denmark's earliest social-housing schemes, born of a cholera epidemic.
facts: Built 1854–1872 by the Danish Medical Association (Lægeforeningen) as cheap, healthy homes for the poor; originally called Lægeforeningens Boliger, the nickname 'Brumleby' became official in 1984.
It was a response to the 1853 Copenhagen cholera outbreak, which killed roughly 5,000 people amid overcrowding and poor sanitation inside the ramparts.

Designed by Michael Gottlieb Bindesbøll in a Neoclassical style and later expanded by Vilhelm Klein; the bicoloured facades (ochre ground floor, white upper) were inspired by Italian agricultural workers' housing.
The flats were small (about 25 sqm) but set new standards for light, air and shared communal facilities.
After a 1990s renovation the apartment count was reduced to about 221; it still functions as social housing.
The name's 'Brumle-' element is thought to echo the sound of cattle that once grazed the surrounding commons, with '-by' meaning town or settlement.
w: Long rows of ochre-and-white cottages, raised by the Medical Association after cholera tore through the cramped city in 1853 and killed some 5,000. Architect Bindesboll gave the poor light, air and 25-square-metre flats when slums were the norm. Still social housing, still standing. Public health you can walk through.

hook: A 16-metre cast-iron-and-glass conservatory with a spiral stair to a treetop catwalk, the centrepiece of the University Botanical Garden's 19th-century glasshouse complex.
facts: The protected Palm House was built 1872–1874 as part of the garden's roughly 3,000 sqm glasshouse complex.
Carlsberg founder J.C. Jacobsen was a driving force behind the project and helped fund it, inspired by London's Crystal Palace of the 1851 Great Exhibition.
It was designed by Copenhagen city architect Peter Christian Bønecke in cast iron and glass.
The central palm hall is about 16 metres tall, with narrow cast-iron spiral stairs leading to a passageway near the roof.
The surrounding Botanical Garden holds Denmark's largest collection of living plants, with well over 10,000 species.
w: A sixteen-metre cathedral of cast iron and glass, with a narrow spiral stair that climbs to a catwalk strung among the palm crowns. Built in the 1870s and bankrolled by brewer J.C. Jacobsen, who'd been dazzled by London's Crystal Palace. Around it sprawls Denmark's largest living plant collection. Climb up and walk the treetops, indoors.

hook: A half-kilometre wedge of park split into a Red Square, a Black Market and a Green Park, furnished with 108 objects gathered from around the world.
facts: Opened in June 2012, designed by the arts group Superflex with architects Bjarke Ingels Group (BIG) and German landscape firm Topotek 1.
Stretches roughly 750 metres along a public cycle track and covers about 30,000 square metres.

Divided into three colour-coded zones: the Red Square (recreation, painted red/orange/pink, including the pink ping-pong tables), the Black Market (a gathering space with fountain) and the Green Park (rolling lawns).

Contains 108 plants and objects sourced from around 62 countries, including a Soviet bus stop, a Moroccan fountain, Iraqi swings, Brazilian benches and English litter bins, each with a bilingual plaque.
Won the 2016 Aga Khan Award for Architecture and a 2013 AIA Honor Award, and was shortlisted for the EU's Mies van der Rohe Award.
w: Half a kilometre of park split into a red square, a black market and a green hill, furnished with 108 objects shipped in from some 62 countries. A Soviet bus stop, a Moroccan fountain, Iraqi swings, pink ping-pong tables, each labelled like a museum piece. Built in 2012 to celebrate Norrebro's mix, and it won the Aga Khan Award.

hook: A monumental yellow-brick church whose west front rises like the pipes of a giant church organ — the masterpiece of Danish Brick Expressionism.
facts: Designed by Peder Vilhelm Jensen-Klint; the foundation stone was laid on 8 September 1921, the tower was inaugurated in 1927 and the church was fully completed in 1940.
Built from around five million yellow bricks, a traditional Danish building material.
The 49-metre (160 ft) bell tower forms a west façade deliberately shaped to evoke a church organ's pipes.
A rare example of expressionist church architecture, considered one of Copenhagen's most distinctive churches.
Commemorates N.F.S. Grundtvig (1783–1872), the Danish pastor, hymn-writer and educator.
w: A west front that rises in fluted ranks like the pipes of a colossal organ, raised from roughly five million yellow Danish bricks. Jensen-Klint's Bispebjerg masterpiece took from 1921 to 1940 to finish and stands as the high point of Brick Expressionism. Named for the hymn-writer Grundtvig. Astonishing up close.

hook: A towering black-and-white painting of huddled rodents by Belgian street artist ROA, looming over a Vesterbro schoolyard.
facts: Painted in June 2015 by the Belgian street artist ROA, known for large monochrome animal works.
Created as part of the 'Surface Project' tied to photographer Søren Solkær's street-art exhibition, when several artists made murals across Copenhagen.
Located on the gable at Gasværksvej (cited as no. 22 / 34) in Vesterbro, facing a fenced schoolyard but visible from the street.
Depicts a clustered group of rodents (rats and similar animals) in ROA's characteristic black-and-white style.
As of 2025 the mural survives, though the lower portion has been tagged with graffiti over the years.
w: A gable end in Vesterbro carries a tower of huddled rodents in stark black and white, the work of Belgian artist ROA, who paints animals huge and unsentimental. He put it up in June 2015 for the Surface Project, alongside Soren Solkaer's street-art show. The base has collected tags over the years; the creatures above still loom.

hook: The last surviving stretch of a defunct Amager freight railway, where volunteers now pedal restored handcars and rail-bikes along the old line.
facts: The Amagerbanen railway opened on 10 July 1907, running from Copenhagen to Dragør on Amager.

Passenger service ended in 1938 (with a wartime revival 1940–47); the rural southern half closed in 1957 for Kastrup Airport's expansion, freight ran until 1991 and the line officially closed in 1995.
The volunteer association Amagerbanens Venner now runs draisines and rail-cycles on the preserved final stretch.
The draisine starting point is at the corner of Uplandsgade and Vermlandsgade (Vermlandsgade 33b), near Kløvermarken, a 5–10 minute walk from Christianshavn metro.
The preserved run is about 1.2 km each way, taking roughly 10–20 minutes per round trip, and operates on open days mainly from spring to autumn.
w: The last surviving stretch of a freight line that crossed Amager from 1907 until the rails fell quiet in 1995. Now the volunteers of Amagerbanens Venner pedal restored handcars along it on open days, a kilometre or so out and back past Klovermarken. Find the start at the Vermlandsgade corner and work your own passage down a dead railway.

hook: A vast royal deer park of ancient oaks and open lawns, roamed by some 2,000 free-ranging deer and crowned by a baroque hunting lodge.
facts: First enclosed as a royal hunting ground in 1669 under Frederik III and developed from 1670 by Christian V; the park covers roughly 11 km² (about 1,100 hectares).
Home to around 2,100 deer — roughly 1,700 fallow, 300 red and 100 sika deer.
At its centre stands the Eremitageslottet (the Hermitage), an 18th-century royal hunting lodge built under Christian VI on the open Eremitagesletten.
Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in July 2015 as part of the 'Par force hunting landscape in North Zealand', for its star-shaped network of hunting avenues.
The most popular entrance is the Klampenborg gate, opposite Klampenborg railway station; the park is famous for huge ancient oak and beech trees.
w: A royal hunting ground enclosed in 1669 and never quite let go of: eleven square kilometres of ancient oaks and open lawn where some 2,000 deer wander free. At its heart sits the baroque Hermitage lodge, alone on the grass. UNESCO listed it in 2015 for its star of old hunting avenues. Walk in from Klampenborg and the deer barely look up.

hook: A brewery gateway carried on the backs of four life-size granite elephants, the signature landmark of the old Carlsberg works.
facts: Completed in 1901 alongside the Ny Carlsberg Brewhouse; the tower is also known as the Elephant Tower (Elefanttårnet).
Designed by architect Vilhelm Dahlerup, with the four granite elephants sculpted by Hans Peder Pedersen-Dan, inspired by Bernini's elephant-and-obelisk in Rome.
Each elephant bears the initials of one of Carl Jacobsen's four surviving children, with the brewery's swastika 'good luck' symbols carved on their flanks.
Carlsberg discontinued commercial use of the swastika in 1940, but the carved symbols remain visible on the elephant sculptures.
The façade carries Carl Jacobsen's motto 'Laboremus pro patria' ('Let us work for our fatherland'); the gate was the historic entrance from the Valby side of the brewery.
w: A brewery gate held up by four granite elephants, each carved with the initials of one of Carl Jacobsen's children and, more awkwardly, with the swastikas Carlsberg used as a luck symbol until 1940. Dahlerup designed the tower in 1901; the beasts nod to Bernini's elephant in Rome. The motto overhead reads, let us work for our country.
